scope: narrow re-validation of pass-1 critical + major fixes at commit 8059275

critical_fixes_confirmed: g3-ce-1: RedbBackend::put_node now fail-closed (system-zone guard at entry, unchecked helper is fn-private). NodeStore::put_node trait delegate routes through the guarded path. Pinned by 3 tests: inherent path, trait path, positive control. All pass.

g3-ce-2: RedbBackend::put_edge and Transaction::put_edge both reject system:-labeled edges. put_edge_unchecked is fn-private. Pinned by 4 tests: inherent, in-transaction, privileged positive control, non-system positive control. All pass.
major_fixes_confirmed: g3-ce-3: Engine::transaction's RefCell replaced with Mutex (ops_cell and user_result). All four lock sites use unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) so poisoning is recoverable. No lock-order hazard: engine-layer Mutexes are released before the backend returns; the graph-layer subscribers Mutex is taken afterwards. Pinned by 2 tests covering closure-panic and policy-panic recovery.

g3-ce-4: PendingOp::DeleteNode now carries labels: Vec<String>. Read-before-delete in Transaction::delete_node reads through the SAME redb WriteTransaction (transaction.rs:339-355) so TOCTOU is impossible under redb's serializable isolation. Idempotent-delete miss produces empty Vec via unwrap_or_default. PendingOp::DeleteEdge symmetrically carries Option<String>. ChangeEvent.labels is a Vec<String> (ordered; preserves Node.labels insertion order). primary_label() and has_label() accessors landed on ChangeEvent.

findings.claim: System-zone prefix check is strictly case-sensitive — 'System:Grant' (capital S) bypasses the guard
findings.detail: All five guard sites use label.starts_with("system:") as a literal ASCII prefix match. A Node with labels = vec!["System:Grant"] or vec!["SYSTEM:Grant"] passes through as a user-zone write. R1 SC1 does not explicitly name case-handling, and the system-zone namespace is otherwise documented in lowercase throughout — but an attacker who knows the guard is case-sensitive can simply title-case their forged label. Since the resulting Node would then be labeled 'System:Grant' (not 'system:Grant'), it would not match legitimate system:CapabilityGrant queries either, so this is not a direct capability-forgery exploit. It IS a namespace-hygiene gap: IVM views or downstream readers doing case-insensitive label matching would see the forged node as a system-grant-adjacent artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) use label.to_ascii_lowercase().starts_with("system:") in all five sites, or (b) add a module-level const SYSTEM_PREFIX: &str = "system:" alongside a comment explicitly stating 'case-sensitive by design; case-variants are not in the system-zone namespace and will not match legitimate system-label queries'. Option (b) is cheaper and preserves the current behavior as intentional. Pick one and pin it with a targeted test (e.g., assert that 'System:Grant' is accepted as a user-zone label but cannot be retrieved via get_by_label("system:Grant")).

findings.claim: policy_panic test verifies recovery on a DIFFERENT engine, not the same one
findings.detail: The closure_panic test uses the SAME engine for the second transaction (line 111) — correctly proves TxGuard RAII released the tx_flag after unwind. The policy_panic test constructs engine2 for the recovery assertion, with an in-code comment explaining this choice ('prove the redb-level state was not corrupted'). This is a strictly weaker contract than the closure-panic case: it does not pin that after a policy panic, engine.tx_flag is released and engine.backend still accepts writes. The TxGuard Drop impl at transaction.rs:182-187 should handle this correctly (it clears the flag unconditionally on drop, and the PoisonError arm unwrap_or_else(e.into_inner()) means a poisoned flag-mutex is still recoverable), but there's no test pin.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third test case: policy_panic_does_not_wedge_the_SAME_engine. Reuse the panic-catching pattern from the existing test but call engine.transaction(...) twice (second call after the first panics). Assert the second call succeeds. This closes the coverage gap without duplicating much code. Alternatively, change the existing test to reuse `engine` and drop `engine2` — but that conflates two assertions. Prefer adding the third test.

findings.claim: put_edge_unchecked's three sequential self.put() calls are non-atomic — body and indexes can land in separate redb transactions
findings.detail: The body write (line 617), source-index write (line 618), and target-index write (line 619) each open their own redb WriteTransaction via the KVBackend::put impl. A process crash between any two of these leaves a partial state: body without indexes, or body + source-index without target-index. The comment acknowledges this ('G3 wraps these in a single redb txn' as a forward-looking note) but the fix has not landed. This is a PRE-EXISTING issue — git show 8059275^ shows the same three-put sequence — so it's out of pass-2 scope for the fix-pass itself. Flagging here because the fix-pass DID touch put_edge to add the guard, so the atomicity gap is adjacent.
findings.suggested_fix: R4b or G5: inline put_edge_unchecked into a single begin_write_txn() + three table.insert() + commit() block, matching the shape of put_node_unchecked (which already does this, lines 452-475). Keep the guard at the entry points unchanged. This is a 10-line refactor with one new test (crash-mid-edge-put leaves no dangling index entries).

findings.claim: Inherent delete_edge mirrors the same non-atomic pattern
findings.detail: delete_edge reads the edge via get_edge (a separate read txn), then calls self.delete() three times — each in its own write transaction. Same crash-recovery gap as g3-p2-ce-3. Pre-existing; not introduced by the fix-pass. Content-addressed invariant does NOT save us here because deletes by CID target the same keys regardless of body content, but the NON-atomicity means the key set can be partially removed.
findings.suggested_fix: Same as g3-p2-ce-3: inline into a single redb txn. Matches the shape of inherent delete_node (lines 528-566).

deferred_items_spotcheck.g3-ce-5 (subscriber-panic dead-letter co: TODO(R4b) at transaction.rs:458 with tracking reference. Acceptable — blocked by tracing dep landing. Stderr breadcrumb at fan_out covers the operator-visibility floor.
deferred_items_spotcheck.g3-ce-7 (TxGuard per-Arc scope): TODO(phase-2) at redb_backend.rs:197-202 documents the limitation (single-handle invariant per-Arc, multi-Arc falls through to redb's single-writer lock which blocks rather than deadlocking). Acceptable — documented fallback is safe.
deferred_items_spotcheck.g3-ce-8 (tx_id reset on reopen): TODO(phase-2) at redb_backend.rs:209-214 names the consequence (IVM persistence-layer monotonicity violation across restart) and the Phase-1 mitigation (IVM rebuilds from scratch). Acceptable — the Phase-1 IVM constraint makes the gap invisible.
deferred_items_spotcheck.g3-ce-10 (register_subscriber ordering): Contract documented at redb_backend.rs:942-950. Operator-facing doc tells IVM authors to register-first-then-snapshot-read. Acceptable for Phase 1; a Subscription handle is filed as g3-cr-15 follow-up.

notes: All four pass-1 critical+major findings are fixed and pinned by passing regression tests (9 tests, 100% pass). The four new findings are all minor: (1) case-sensitivity in the system-zone prefix match — a namespace-hygiene concern, not a capability-forgery exploit; (2) a coverage gap in engine_transaction_policy_panic_safe.rs where the policy-panic recovery test uses a fresh engine instead of the panicking one; (3,4) pre-existing non-atomicity in put_edge_unchecked and delete_edge — not regressions, but adjacent to the fix-pass surface and worth calling out. None block G5 dispatch. Verdict: accept-with-minors — land the fix-pass, file the four minors on the R4b docket. The critical system-zone bypass is closed; the panic-safety is Mutex-recoverable; the delete ChangeEvents carry labels via atomic read-before-delete under redb's serializable isolation.
